S. |year=1978 |title=Recoveries of Saskatchewan-banded Great Horned Owls |journal=Canadian Field-Naturalist |volume=92 |pages=61–66|doi=10.5962/p.346633 |doi-access=free }}</ref> but there is no annual migration even at the northern limits of the great horned owl's range.<ref name= Rohner/>

===Hunting behavior===
[[File:0 Bubo virginianus - Grand-duc d'Amérique (1).JPG|thumb|right|His piercing yellow eyes and his ear tufts.]]
[[File:Claws1a.jpg|thumb|right|Closeup of great horned owl toes and talons]]
Hunting tends to peak between 8:30 pm and midnight and then can resume from 4:30 am to sunrise.<ref name= Smith2>{{cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=D. G. |first2=J. R. |last2=Murphy |year=1973 |title=Breeding ecology of raptors in the eastern Great Basin of Utah |journal=Brigham Young University Science Bulletin Biological Services |volume=18 |issue=3 |pages=1–76 |url=https://scholarsarchive.byu.edu/byuscib/vol18/iss3/1}}</ref> Hunting tends to be most prolonged during winter by virtue of prey being more scarce.<ref>{{cite thesis |last1=Fuller |first1=M. R. |year=1979 |title=Spatiotemporal ecology of four sympatric raptor species |type=Ph.D. |publisher=Univ. of Minnesota |location=St. Paul}}</ref>  However, great horned owls can learn to target certain prey during daylight in the afternoon when it is more vulnerable, such as [[Fox squirrel|eastern fox squirrels]] (''Sciurus niger'') while they are building their leaf nests and [[Sauromalus ater|chuckawallas]] (''Sauromalus ater'') sunning themselves on desert rocks.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Packard |first1=R. L. |year=1954 |title=Great horned owl attacking squirrel nests |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=66 |issue=4 |page=272 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v066n04/p0272-p0272.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vaughan |first1=T.A. |year=1954 |title=Diurnal foraging by the Great Horned Owl |journal=Wilson Bulletin |volume=66 |issue=2 |page=148 |jstor=4158310}}</ref> Owls hunt mainly by watching from a snag, pole or other high perch. During hunting forays, they often fly about {{convert|50|to|100|m|ft|abbr=on}} from perch to perch, stopping to survey for food at each, until they sense a prey item below. From such vantage points, owls dive down to the ground, often with wings folded, to ambush their prey.<ref name=Konig/> Effective maximum hunting distance of an owl from an elevated perch is {{convert|90|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Petersen/> Due to their short but broad wings, great horned owls are ideally suited for low speed and maneuverability.<ref name= Johnson/>

Despite reports that they do not hunt on the wing,<ref name="Rudolph">{{cite journal |last1=Rudolph |first1=S. G. |year=1978 |title=Predation Ecology of Coexistng Great Horned and Barn Owls |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=90 |issue=1 |pages=134–137 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v090n01/p0134-p0137.pdf}}</ref> they also sometimes hunt by flying low over openings on the ground, scanning below for prey activity.<ref name="Konig" /> Great horned owls can fly at speeds of more than {{convert|65|km/h|mph|abbr=on}} in level flight.<ref name="Voous" /> Hunting flights are slow, often quartering low above the ground where prey is likely to occur in open country or open woodland. Brief hovering flight (for about 6–18 seconds) has been described, especially in windy areas.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=D. G. |first2=B. A. |last2=Smith |year=1972 |title=Hunting methods and success of newly-fledged Great Horned Owls |journal=Bird-Banding |volume=43 |issue=2 |page=142 |doi=10.2307/4511863 |jstor=4511863 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/50297|url-access=subscription }}</ref> On occasion owls may actually walk on the ground in pursuit of small prey or, rarely, inside a chicken coop to prey on the fowl within.<ref name="owlpages.com" /> [[Rodent]]s and [[invertebrate]]s may be caught on foot around the base of bushes, through grassy areas, and near culverts and other human structures in range and farm habitat.<ref name="Houston" /> The great horned owl is generally a poor walker on the ground; it walks like a [[starling]], with a pronounced side-to-side gait. They have been known to wade into shallow water for aquatic prey, although this has been only rarely reported.<ref name="Houston" /> Owls can snatch [[bird]]s and some arboreal mammals directly from tree branches in a glide as well. The stiff feathering of their wings allows owls to produce minimal sound in flight while hunting.<ref name="Cornell" /><ref name="Konig" /><ref name="owlpages.com" />

Almost all prey are killed by crushing with the owl's feet or by incidentally stabbing of the talons, though some may be bitten about the face as well. Prey is swallowed whole when possible. When prey is swallowed whole, owls regurgitate [[Pellet (ornithology)|pellets]] of bone and other non-digestible bits about 6 to 10 hours later, usually in the same location where the prey was consumed.<ref name="Konig" /> Great horned owl pellets are dark gray or brown in color and very large, {{convert|7.6|to|10.2|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and {{convert|3.8|cm|in|abbr=on}} thick, and have been known to contain skulls up to {{convert|3|cm|in|abbr=on}} in width inside them.<ref name="owlpages.com" /> However, not all prey can be swallowed at once, and owls will also fly with prey to a perch and tear off pieces with their bill. Most dietary studies focus on pellets found under perches and around nests, since they provide a more complete picture of the diversity of prey consumed, but prey remains outside of pellets may provide clues to prey excluded from the pellets and a combination of both is recommended.<ref name="Voous" /><ref name="Hamerstrom" /><ref name="Orians">{{cite journal |last1=Orians |first1=G. |last2=Kuhlman |first2=F. |year=1956 |title=Red-tailed hawk and horned owl populations in Wisconsin |journal=The Condor |volume=58 |issue=5 |pages=371–385 |doi=10.2307/1365056|jstor=1365056 }}</ref>

Many large prey items are dismembered. Great horned owls may behead large prey before taking it to its nest or eating perch. The legs may also be removed, as may (in some bird prey) the wings. The great horned owl will also crush the bones of its prey to make it more compact for carrying.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Einarsen |first1=A. S. |year=1956 |title=Determination of some predator species by food signs |journal=Oregon State Monographs |volume=10 |page=34}}</ref> On occasion, the owls may return to the kill site to continue eating if the prey is too heavy to fly with after dismemberment.<ref name="Bent" /> Many owls will accrue a cache of prey, especially those who are nesting. Caches must be at a safe location, usually the crotch of a tall tree. In northern regions, where large prey is prevalent, an owl may let uneaten food freeze and then thaw it out later using its own body heat.<ref name="Mikkola" />  Hunting success seems to require fairly open understory, and experimental testing of microhabitat proved that open areas provided more hunting success on five species of rodent, with cloudy nights and denser bush foliage both decreasing success.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Longland |first1=W. S. |first2=M. V. |last2=Price |year=1991 |title=Direct observations of owls and heteromyid rodents: can predation risk explain microhabitat use? |journal=Ecology |volume=72 |issue=6 |pages=2261–2273 |doi=10.2307/1941576|jstor=1941576 |bibcode=1991Ecol...72.2261L }}</ref>

==Prey and trophic ecology==
 [[File:Bubo virginianusDO1908P01CA.JPG|thumb|right|upright|Painting by [[Louis Agassiz Fuertes]] depicting a great horned owl with one of its primary prey species, a [[snowshoe hare]]]]

Prey can vary greatly based on opportunity. According to one author, "Almost any living creature that walks, crawls, flies, or swims, except the large mammals, is the great horned owl's legitimate prey".<ref name=Lubbock/> In fact, the great horned owl has the most diverse prey profile of any raptor in the Americas.<ref name= Voous/> Over 500 species have been identified as great horned owl prey, with dozens more identified only to genus or general type (especially numerous [[invertebrate]]s) and presumably several more unknown from their relatively little-studied populations in the [[Neotropical realm|Neotropic]]s. [[Mammal]]s (more than 200 species) and [[bird]]s (nearly 300 species) make up the majority of their diet.<ref name= Voous/><ref name= Hamerstrom>{{cite journal |last1=Errington |first1=P. L. |first2=F. |last2=Hamerstrom |first3=F. N. Jr. |last3=Hamerstrom |year=1940 |title=The Great Horned Owl and its prey in north-central United States |journal=Iowa Agricultural Express Research Bulletin |volume=24 |issue=277 |pages=757–850 |url=https://dr.lib.iastate.edu/handle/20.500.12876/103885}}</ref> Their diet in North America is made up of 87.6% mammals, 6.1% birds, 1.6% [[reptile]]s and [[amphibian]]s with the remaining 4.7% being made up by insects, other assorted [[invertebrate]]s and [[fish]].<ref name= Voous/>

Estimated mass of individual prey for the owls has ranged from as little as {{convert|0.4|g|oz|abbr=on}} to as much as {{convert|6.8|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="Cromrich">{{cite journal |last1=Cromrich |first1=L. A. |last2=Holt |first2=D. W. |last3=Leasure |first3=S. M. |year=2002 |title=Trophic niche of North American great horned owls |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=36 |issue=1 |pages=58–65 |url=https://biostor.org/reference/216176}}</ref><ref name="Murphy">{{cite journal |last1=Murphy |first1=R. K. |year=1997 |title=Importance of prairie wetlands and avian prey to breeding Great Horned Owls (Bubo virginianus) in northwestern North Dakota |journal=United States Department of Agricultural, Forest Service General Technical Report |pages=286–298}}</ref> Most prey is in the range of {{convert|4|g|oz|abbr=on}} ([[shrew]]s) to {{convert|2300|g|lb|abbr=on}} ([[Hare|jackrabbit]]s).<ref name="Cromrich" /><ref name="Jaksic2">{{cite journal |last1=Jaksić |first1=F. M. |last2=Marti |first2=C. D. |year=1984 |title=Comparative food habits of Bubo owls in Mediterranean-type ecosystems |journal=The Condor |volume=86 |issue=3 |pages=288–296 |doi=10.2307/1366997|jstor=1366997 |doi-access=free }}</ref> A single owl requires about {{convert|50|to|100|g|oz|abbr=on}} of food per day and can subsist on a large kill over several days.<ref name="DwightSmith">{{cite book|last=Smith|first=Dwight G.|title=Great Horned Owl|year=2002|publisher=Stackpole Books|location=Mechanicsburg, PA |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=f1PVJVWbQKwC |isbn=978-0-8117-2689-4 |pages=33, 80–81 |edition=1st|access-date=2013-03-21}}</ref> Despite the great diversity of prey taken by these predators, in most of the continental United States from the East to the Midwest as well as [[Canada]] and [[Alaska]], great horned owls largely live off just a handful of prey species: three species of [[Lagomorpha|lagomorph]]: the [[eastern cottontail]] (''Sylvilagus floridanus''), the [[snowshoe hare]] (''Lepus americanus'') and the [[black-tailed jackrabbit]] (''Lepus californicus''); two species of [[Peromyscus|New World mice]]: the [[white-footed mouse]] and the [[Peromyscus maniculatus|North American deermouse]] (''Peromyscus leucopus & maniculatus''); approximately three species of [[vole]]: the [[Meadow vole|meadow]], [[Prairie vole|prairie]] and [[woodland vole]]s (''Microtus pennsylvanicus, ochrogaster & pinetorum''); and one introduced pest, the brown rat.<ref name="Houston" /><ref name="Hamerstrom" /><ref name="Cromrich" />

===Rodents===
[[File:White-footed Mouse, Cantley, Quebec.jpg|thumb|right|A large portion of the great horned owl's food consists of small rodents, such as [[white-footed mouse|white-footed mice]].]]
Small [[rodent]]s form the great majority of great horned owl prey by number. Weighing a mere {{convert|14|to|31.5|g|oz|abbr=on}} and {{convert|20|to|58|g|oz|abbr=on}} on average, the nine species of New World mice in ''Peromyscus'' and eight species voles in ''Microtus'' recorded in the diet would appear to be overly small to be as important as they are to a predatory bird of this size. The prominence of these genera is undoubtedly due to the abundance of both genera in the wooded edge habitats frequented by great horned owls. It is estimated that a family of owls with two offspring would need to take about a half dozen (voles) to a dozen (mice) of these rodents every night to satisfy their dietary requirements but apparently the accessibility and abundance of these foods is irresistible as their numeric dominance is indisputable.<ref name= Craighead/><ref name= Hamerstrom/> By winter in areas that hold heavy snow, ''[[Peromyscus]]'' mice often come to outnumber the voles in the diet since the mice tend to travel over the surface of the snow while the voles make tunnels underneath the snow.<ref name= Craighead/><ref name= Voous/><ref name= Hamerstrom/> In fact, a healthy family of great horned owls can decimate a colony of field rats, thus potentially performing a key role in controlling a highly destructive pest.<ref name= Hamerstrom/>  Great horned owls living in the timbered fringes of garbage or refuse dumps may subsist mostly on rats.<ref name= Mikkola/>

In the [[Rocky Mountains|Rockies]], California, the [[Southwestern United States]] and northern [[Mexico]], the diversity of this species' diet rises, in sync with the diversity of rodents. Especially important, from [[Colorado]] to [[Washington (state)|Washington state]] is the [[northern pocket gopher]] (''Thomomys talpoides''), although assorted other pocket gophers (''[[Geomys]], [[Cratogeomys]], [[Michoacan pocket gopher|Zygogeomys]], [[Pappogeomys]]'' and other ''[[Thomomys]]'' ssp.) are readily taken. While the northern weighs from {{convert|90|to|120|g|oz|abbr=on}}, other pocket gophers hunted average from {{convert|95|to|545|g|lb|abbr=on}} in mass. From [[Washington (state)|Washington]] to [[Baja California]] a very important food is the [[Heteromyidae|pocket mice]], primarily the [[Great Basin pocket mouse]] (''Perognathus parvus''). While the Great Basin species is a relative giant at {{convert|22|g|oz|abbr=on}}, other hunted pocket mice (which may include both ''[[Perognathus]]'' and ''[[Chaetodipus]]'' ssp.) can average nearly as light as {{convert|8|g|oz|abbr=on}}. In [[East Texas]], the {{convert|159|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[hispid cotton rat]] (''Sigmodon hispidus'') is the most commonly recorded prey species.<ref name= Parmalee>{{cite journal |last1=Parmalee |first1=P. W. |year=1954 |title=Food of the great horned owl and barn owl in east Texas |journal=The Auk |volume=71 |issue=4 |pages=469–470 |doi=10.2307/4081546|jstor=4081546 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The same species constituted 75% by number of a small sampling in [[Oklahoma]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Baumgartner |first1=A. M. |last2=Baumgartner |first2=F. M. |year=1944 |title=Hawks and owls in Oklahoma 1939–1942: Food habits and population changes |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=56 |issue=4 |pages=209–215 |jstor=4157378}}</ref> In semi-desert and other arid habitats, [[kangaroo rat]]s become increasingly important prey, ten species have been reported in the diet but most prominently the [[Ord's kangaroo rat|Ord's]]  and [[Merriam's kangaroo rat]]s (''Dipodomys ordii & merriami''), both being widespread, numerous and relatively diminutive (at {{convert|42|and|48|g|oz|abbr=on}}). Eight known larger species of kangaroo rats, including the [[giant kangaroo rat]] (''Dipodomys ingens'') averaging at {{convert|152|g|oz|abbr=on}}, are also taken.<ref name= Fitch>{{cite journal |last1=Fitch |first1=H. S. |year=1947 |title=Predation by owls in the Sierran foothills of California |journal=The Condor |volume=49 |issue=4 |pages=137–151 |doi=10.2307/1364108|jstor=1364108 }}</ref><ref name= Murphy2>{{cite journal |last1=Murphy |first1=J. R. |last2=Camenzind |first2=F. J. |last3=Smith |first3=D. G. |last4=Weston |first4=J. B. |year=1969 |title=Nesting ecology of raptorial birds in central Utah |journal=Brigham Young University Science Bulletin-Biological Series |volume=10 |issue=4 |pages=1–36 |url=https://scholarsarchive.byu.edu/byuscib/vol10/iss4/1}}</ref><ref name= Fitch2>{{cite journal |last1=Fitch |first1=H. S. |year=1940 |title=Some observations on horned owl nests |journal=The Condor |volume=42 |issue=1 |pages=73–75 |doi=10.2307/1364321|jstor=1364321 }}</ref>

The [[squirrel]]s, including [[ground squirrel]]s, [[marmot]]s (''Marmota''), [[prairie dog]]s (''Cynomys''), [[chipmunk]]s and [[tree squirrel]]s, are diurnal and so are largely unavailable to great horned owls as prey. Occasionally though, one will be caught from their leaf nest, nest hole or burrow entrance first thing in the morning or in the late afternoon and approximately 35 species have been successfully predated by these owls. In general larger sized than other rodent families, the species hunting range from the {{convert|62|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[gray-collared chipmunk]] (''Tamias cinereicollis'') to the {{convert|5775|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[hoary marmot]] (''Marmota caligata''); thus, squirrels can provide a very fulfilling meal.<ref name= Bent/><ref name= Houston/><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.birdnote.org/birdnote-transcript.cfm?id=1529 |title=Great Horned Owl Menu |publisher=Birdnote.org |date=2008-08-29 |access-date=2012-02-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120310184307/http://www.birdnote.org/birdnote-transcript.cfm?id=1529 |archive-date=2012-03-10 }}</ref>  An even larger rodent is sometimes attacked as prey by great horned owls, the [[North American porcupine]] (''Erethizon dorsatum''), in which average adults range from {{convert|4500|to|9000|g|lb|abbr=on}}. This has been determined from owls who have porcupine quills imbedded in them, sometimes resulting in death.<ref name= Bent/><ref name= Powell>{{cite book |last1=Powell |first1=B. |year=1984 |title=Labrador by choice |publisher=Jesperson Press |location=St. John's, NF |isbn=0-920502-31-8}}</ref> On occasion, they are successful in killing porcupine, even adults as determined by the size of the quills left behind and prey remains at bloodied kill sites.<ref name=DwightSmith/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Eifrig |first1=H. |year=1909 |title=Great horned owl versus porcupine |journal=The Auk |volume=26 |pages=58–59 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SZ5-ZWceXswC&dq=%22Great+horned+owl+versus+porcupine%22+auk&pg=PA58}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wiley |first1=J.W. |year=1969 |title=A Case of Great Horned Owl Predation on a Porcupine |journal=The Condor |volume=71 |issue=1 |page=73 |doi=10.2307/1366054|jstor=1366054 }}</ref> Other rodents recorded as secondary prey in North America include [[New World flying squirrel|flying squirrels]] (''Glaucomys'' ssp.), the [[golden mouse]] (''Ochrotomys nuttalli''), [[Clethrionomys|red-backed vole]]s & [[bog lemming]]s (''Myodes'' & ''Synaptomys'' ssp.), the [[muskrat]] (''Ondatra zibethicus''), the [[northern grasshopper mouse]] (''Onychomys leucogaster''), the [[northern pygmy mouse]] (''Baiomys taylori'') and [[Jumping mouse|jumping mice]] (''Zapus'' & ''Napaeozapus'' ssp.).<ref name= Bent/><ref name= Houston/>

===Lagomorphs===
[[File:Black tailed jackrabbit.jpg|thumb|right|upright|[[Black-tailed jackrabbit]]s are an important food source for western great horned owls.]] 
Although generally no match for rodents in the sheer quantity of individuals, in terms of prey biomass, the most significant prey of North American great horned owls are [[hare]]s and [[rabbit]]s. About a dozen lagomorphs species are known to be hunted by the owl, from the relatively tiny {{convert|420|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[pygmy rabbit]] to adult [[jackrabbit]]s weighing more than {{convert|2000|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Bent/><ref>Dobler, Frederick C., and Kenneth R. Dixon. "The pygmy rabbit Brachylagus idahoensis." Rabbits, hares, and pikas: status survey and conservation action plan (JA Chapman and JEC Flux, eds.). IUCN/SSC Lagomorph Specialist Group, Gland, Switzerland (1990): 111–115.</ref> These species are overall the largest regular prey for this species.<ref name= Voous/>

In [[Utah]], where great horned owls are dependent on the jackrabbits, average brood size rose from 2 at jackrabbit population lows to 3.3 when the jackrabbits were at their peak. At the peak of the population cycle, jackrabbits accounted for 90.2% and [[desert cottontail]]s (''Sylvilagus audubonii'') for another 8.7% of prey biomass.<ref name="Smith2" /><ref name="Smith3">{{cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=D. G. |first2=J. R. |last2=Murphy |year=1979 |title=Breeding responses of raptors to jackrabbit density in the eastern Great Basin Desert of Utah |journal=Raptor Research |volume=13 |pages=1–14 |url=https://archive.org/details/biostor-216510}}</ref> In the short-grass prairie of [[Colorado]], [[mountain cottontail]] (''Sylvilagus nuttallii'') and black-tailed jackrabbits predominated in October to December, making up 42.9% by number (and nearly all the biomass), thence dropping to 9.3% by number in April, while voles rose to 32.2% peak in May, down to a minimum of 10.2% by number in June.<ref name="Voous" /> Further north in Colorado, in the absence of jackrabbits, the mountain cottontails falls to third place by number (12.9%) behind the northern pocket gopher (36.5%) and prairie vole (24.7%) but still dominates the biomass, making up about half.<ref name="Marti2">{{cite journal |last1=Marti |first1=C. D. |year=1969 |title=Some comparisons of the feeding ecology of four owls in north-central Colorado |journal=The Southwestern Naturalist |volume=14 |issue=2 |pages=163–170 |doi=10.2307/3668871|jstor=3668871 |bibcode=1969SWNat..14..163M }}</ref> 

In central Utah, the lagomorphs (black-tailed jackrabbit/desert cottontail) and Ord's kangaroo rat each made up 39% of the food by number, respectively.<ref name="Smith2" /> The mountain cottontail dominates the biomass of prey in the [[Sierra Nevada (U.S.)|Sierran foothills]] of California, making up 61.1% of the biomass, although are numerically secondary to desert woodrat.<ref name="Fitch" /> Remarkably, in the [[Morley Nelson Snake River Birds of Prey National Conservation Area]] of [[Idaho]], individual rodents (1159 counted) were more than 10 times more numerous than lagomorphs (114 counted) by quantity and yet the jackrabbit and mountain cottontail still made up approximately half of the biomass.<ref name="Marti, C. D. 1996">{{cite journal |last1=Marti |first1=C. D. |last2=Kochert |first2=M. N. |year=1996 |title=Diet and Trophic Characteristics of Great Horned Owls in Southwestern Idaho |journal=Journal of Field Ornithology |volume=67 |issue=4 |pages=499–506 |jstor=4514149|bibcode=1996JFOrn..67..499M }}</ref> The dependence on lagomorphs also extends into [[Mexico]], as in [[Baja California]] about a quarter of identified prey was black-tailed jackrabbit and either desert or the larger [[Mexican cottontail]] (''Sylvilagus cunicularius'').<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Llinas-Gutirrez |first1=J. |last2=Arnaud |first2=G. |last3=Acevedo |first3=M. |year=1991 |title=Food habits of the Great Horned Owl in the Cape Region of Lower California, Mexico |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=25 |issue=4 |pages=140–141}}</ref>

In the northern [[Taiga|boreal forest]], great horned owls are even more dependent on the snowshoe hare. At the peak of the 10-year hare cycle, snowshoe hares were by far the largest component of both summer and winter diets (77–81% and 90–99%, respectively, in [[Alberta]]; 83–86% and 75–98%, respectively, in [[Yukon]]). At the lowest point of the hare's cycle, summer diets consisted of only 0–16% snowshoe hare in Alberta and 12.7% in Yukon. When hares were scarce, great horned owls in these regions fed mostly on large [[rodent]]s, mice and voles, [[grouse]] and [[duck]]s. Because fewer of these alternative prey species are available in boreal forests during winter, owls had to emigrate or suffer high mortalities if they stayed.<ref name="Adamcik">{{cite journal |last1=Adamcik |first1=R. S. |first2=A. W. |last2=Todd |first3=L. B. |last3=Keith |year=1978 |title=Demographic and dietary responses of Great Horned Owls during a snowshoe hare cycle |journal=Canadian Field-Naturalist |volume=92 |issue=2 |pages=156–166 |doi=10.5962/p.346661 |issn=0008-3550|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rohner |first1=C. |year=1995 |title=Great Horned Owls and snowshoe hares: what causes the time lag in the numerical response of predators to cyclic prey? |journal=Oikos |volume=74 |issue=1 |pages=61–68 |doi=10.2307/3545675|jstor=3545675 |bibcode=1995Oikos..74...61R }}</ref> In [[Alberta]], the local population of great horned owls can increase threefold from hare population lows to peaks.<ref name="Mcinvaille">{{cite journal |last1=Mcinvaille |first1=W. B. Jr. |first2=L. B. |last2=Keith |year=1974 |title=Predator-prey relations and breeding biology of the Great Horned Owl and Red-tailed Hawk in central Alberta |journal=Canadian Field-Naturalist |volume=88 |pages=1–20|doi=10.5962/p.344317 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The dependency on the snowshoe hare by the great horned owl extends into [[Alaska]] as well.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Weir |first1=D. |first2=A. |last2=Hanson |year=1989 |title=Food habits of Great Horned Owls, Bubo virginianus, in the northern taiga of the Yukon Territory and Alaska |journal=Canadian Field-Naturalist |volume=103 |pages=12–17|doi=10.5962/p.356045 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

===Other mammals===
Other mammals are taken readily as well. Several species of [[Carnivora|mammalian carnivore]], such as [[Ring-tailed cat|ringtail]]s (''Bassariscus astutus''), [[American mink]]s (''Neogale vison''), [[American marten]]s (''Martes americana''), [[black-footed ferret]] (''Mustela nigripes'') and various other small [[mustelid]]s (''Mustela'' ssp.), are sometimes taken as prey.<ref name="Bent" /><ref name="Houston" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bull |first1=E. L. |last2=Heater |first2=T. W. |year=2001 |title=Survival, Causes of Mortality, and Reproduction in the American Marten in Northeastern Oregon|journal=Northwestern Naturalist |volume=82 |issue=1 |pages=1–6|doi=10.2307/3536640 |jstor=3536640 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mendall |first1=H. L. |year=1944 |title=Food of Hawks and Owls N Maine |journal=The Journal of Wildlife Management |volume=8 |issue=3 |pages=198–208 |doi=10.2307/3795698|jstor=3795698 }}</ref> In one case, a great horned owl was the likely killer of an adult female [[Fisher (animal)|fisher]] (''Martes pennanti''), though young ones are typically taken.<ref>Roger A. Powell, ''Martes pennanti'', "Mammalian Species", Issue 156, 8 May 1981, pp. 1–6, {{dot|10.2307/3504050}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Axelson |first1=G. |year=2012 |title=Fewer Fishers in the Forest |publisher=Minnesota Department of Natural Resources}}</ref> Prey in the form of [[Canidae|canids]], like [[fox]]es or [[coyote]]s (''Canis latrans'') are often juveniles presumably snatched from the mouths of dens by night.<ref name="Bent" /><ref name="Houston" /><ref>{{cite book |last1=Brown |first1=L. N. |year=1997 |title=A guide to the mammals of the southeastern United States |publisher=University of Tennessee Press |isbn= 0-87049-966-1}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Storm |first1=G. L. |last2=Andrews |first2=R. D. |last3=Phillips |first3=R. L. |last4=Bishop |first4=R. A. |last5=Siniff |first5=D. B. |last6=Tester |first6=J. R. |year=1976 |title=Morphology, reproduction, dispersal, and mortality of midwestern red fox populations |journal=Wildlife Monographs |issue=49 |pages=3–82 |jstor=3830425}}</ref><ref name="Ray">{{cite web |last1=Ray |first1=J.D. |year=2012 |title=Great Silent Hunter |website=Birdwatching Magazine |location=Braintree, Massachusetts |url=https://www.birdwatchingdaily.com/news/species-profiles/great-silent-hunter/}}</ref> [[Kit fox|Kit]] and [[swift fox]]es of up to adult size may be taken.<ref name="DwightSmith" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sovada |first1=M. A. |last2=Roy |first2=C. C. |last3=Bright |first3=J. B. |last4=Gillis |first4=J. R. |year=1998 |title=Causes and rates of mortality of swift foxes in western Kansas |journal=The Journal of Wildlife Management |volume=62 |issue=4 |pages=1300–1306 |doi=10.2307/3801994|jstor=3801994 |bibcode=1998JWMan..62.1300S }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kilgore |first1=D.L. |year=1969 |title=An ecological study of the swift fox (Vulpes velox) in the Oklahoma panhandle |journal=American Midland Naturalist |volume=81 |issue=2 |pages=513–534 |doi=10.2307/2423986|jstor=2423986 }}</ref>

The most regular predatory association amongst relatively larger carnivores is that with skunks. Due to their poor sense of smell, great horned owls are the only predators to routinely attack these bold mammals with impunity. All six skunk species found in North America are reported as prey, including full-grown [[striped skunk]]s (''Mephitis mephitis''), which can be three times as heavy as the attacking owl.<ref name="Bent" /><ref name="Cromrich" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Huey |first1=L. M. |year=1931 |title=Skunks as Prey for Owls |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |page=224 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v043n03/p0224-p0224.pdf}}</ref> In one single nest, the remains of 57 striped skunks were found.<ref name="Hunter_Carnivores">{{cite book|last=Hunter|first=Luke|title=Carnivores of the World|year=2011|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|isbn=978-0-691-15228-8}}</ref> Due to the proclivity of skunk predation, great horned owls nests frequently smell strongly of skunk and occasionally stink so powerfully of skunk that they leave the smell at kill sites or on prey remains.<ref name="Houston" /><ref name="Crocoll">{{cite thesis |last1=Crocoll |first1=S. T. |year=1984 |title=Breeding biology of broad-winged and red-shouldered hawks in western New York |type=Doctoral dissertation |publisher=State University of New York at Fredonia, Department of Biology |url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/53153}}</ref> Surprisingly, at least two cases of a great horned owl preying on an adult [[raccoon]] (''Procyon lotor'') have been reported.<ref name="Bosakowski">{{cite journal |last1=Bosakowski |first1=T. |first2=R. |last2=Speiser |first3=D. G. |last3=Smith |year=1989 |title=Nesting ecology of forest-dwelling Great Horned Owls in the Eastern Deciduous Forest Biome |journal=Canadian Field-Naturalist |volume=103 |pages=65–69|doi=10.5962/p.356054 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://mariewin.server304.com/johnblog/2005/07/blakeman-answers-questions-about-nest.html |title=Blakeman answers questions about nest maintenance |publisher=Blakeman on Hawks |access-date=6 July 2005 |archive-date=4 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304040936/http://mariewin.server304.com/johnblog/2005/07/blakeman-answers-questions-about-nest.html }}</ref> One instance of an owl taking a [[bobcat]] (''Lynx rufus'') as prey was also reportedly observed.<ref name="DwightSmith" />  Occasionally, domestic carnivores are also prey. A few cases of young or small [[dog]]s (''Canis lupus familiaris'')<ref name="CNN_chihuahua">{{cite web|last=Olson|first=Karen|title=Chihuahua survives owl attack in Illinois|url=http://www.cnn.com/2011/US/01/19/illinois.dog.attacked/index.html?hpt=C2|publisher=CNN|access-date=21 March 2013|date=January 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last=Johansson|first=Tait|url=http://www.bedfordaudubon.org/birds/ghow01.html|title=The Great Horned Owl|publisher=Bedford Audubon Society|access-date=2012-02-09|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120320195411/http://www.bedfordaudubon.org/birds/ghow01.html|archive-date=2012-03-20}}</ref> and several of juvenile and adult [[cat]]s (''Felis silvestris catus'') being killed by great horned owls have been reported.<ref name="Bent" /><ref name="Ray" /><ref>Forbush, Edward H. "The Great Horned Owl (1927)." The Essential Naturalist: Timeless Readings in Natural History (2011): 15.</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Deal |first1=K. |year=2010 |title=Wildlife and Natural Resource Management |publisher=Cengage Learning |isbn=978-1-305-62774-1}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Beware of the Great Horned Owl|url=http://helpfindlostpets.com/birds/beware-of-the-great-horned-owl|publisher=Help Find Lost Pets|access-date=21 March 2013|date=May 27, 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120303102227/http://helpfindlostpets.com/birds/beware-of-the-great-horned-owl|archive-date=3 March 2012}}</ref><ref name="RaptorCenter">{{cite web|title=Great Horned Owl – Bubo virginianus|url=http://www.raptorcenter.org/great-horned-owl.asp|publisher=Raptor Education Center|access-date=21 March 2013}}</ref>

Aside from carnivorans, various other mammals are taken as supplemental prey. At least eight species of [[shrew]]s are taken by opportunity and make up the smallest mammalian prey taken by great horned owls, as specimens of [[least shrew]] (''Cryptotis parva'') or [[masked shrew]] (''Sorex cinereus'') have had an estimated weight of only {{convert|2|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Cromrich/> One of the more regularly taken shrews, though, is the larger {{convert|19.5|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[northern short-tailed shrew]] (''Blarina brevicauda''), which was represented in more than 2% of pellets in the [[Upper Midwest]].<ref name="Hamerstrom" /> [[Mole (animal)|Mole]]s, of at least four or five species, are also widely but lightly reported as prey.<ref name="Hamerstrom" /><ref name="Cromrich" /> From both the tropics and the United States, several species of opossum may be taken, down to the size of the tiny [[dwarf fat-tailed mouse opossum]] (''Thylamys velutinus'').<ref>James T. Pokines "Prey Remains from a Great Horned Owl (Bubo virginianus) Roost in the Icla Valley, Bolivia," Journal of Raptor Research 41(2), 174–175, (1 June 2007). {{doi|10.3356/0892-1016(2007)41[174:PRFAGH]2.0.CO;2}}</ref> In [[Brazil]], juvenile [[white-eared opossum]] (''Didelphis albiventris'') weighing around {{convert|1000|g|lb|abbr=on}} were found in 12% of pellets weighing about.<ref>Tomazzoni, Ana C., Ezequiel Pedó, and Sandra M. Hartz. "Food habits of Great Horned Owls (Bubo virginianus) in the breeding season in Lami Biological Reserve, southern Brazil." Ornitologia Neotropical 15.2 (2004): 279–282.</ref> North American subspecies can prey on larger [[Virginia opossum]] (''Didelphis virginianus'') readily, and can be a major predator of this species.<ref>Gardner, Alfred L. "Virginia opossum." Wild mammals of North America: biology, management, and economics. Johns Hopkins University Press, Baltimore, MD (1982): 3–36.</ref><ref name="Wink">{{cite journal |last1=Wink |first1=J.E. |last2=Senner |first2=S. E. |last3=Goodrich |first3=L. J. |year=1987 |title=Food habits of great horned owls in Pennsylvania |journal=Proceedings of the Pennsylvania Academy of Science |volume=61 |issue=2 |pages=133–137 |jstor=44111738}}</ref> Remnants of [[armadillo]], presumably [[nine-banded armadillo]] (''Dasypus novemcinctus''), have been found around owl nests in the south.<ref name="Bent" /> 11 species of bat are known to be hunted by great horned owls.<ref name="Voous" /><ref name="Burns">{{cite journal |last1=Burns |first1=B. J. |year=1952 |title=Food of a Family of Great Horned Owls, Bubo virginianus, in Florida |journal=The Auk |volume=69 |issue=1 |pages=86–87 |doi=10.2307/4081300|jstor=4081300 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Baker |first1=J. K. |year=1962 |title=The manner and efficiency of raptor depredations on bats |journal=The Condor |volume=64 |issue=6 |pages=500–504 |doi=10.2307/1365473|jstor=1365473 }}</ref><ref name="Kittredge">{{cite journal |last1=Kittredge |first1=V. C. |last2=Wilson |first2=P. W. |last3=Caire |first3=W. |year=2007 |title=An updated checklist of the food items of the great horned owl (Bubo virginianus: Strigiformes: Strigidae) in Oklahoma |journal=Proceedings- Oklahoma Academy of Science |volume=86 |url=https://ojs.library.okstate.edu/osu/index.php/OAS/article/view/5811}}</ref> One pellet in [[Texas]] was found to be composed entirely of [[Mexican free-tailed bat]]s (''Tadarida brasiliensis'').<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Twente |first1=J. W. |year=1954 |title=Predation on bats by hawks and owls |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=66 |issue=2 |pages=135–136 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/127345}}</ref>

===Birds===
[[File:American Coot Image 001.jpg|thumb|right|[[American coot]]s are often a favored food source for great horned owls living near wetlands.]]
After mammals, birds rank as the next most important general prey group. Birds are usually considerably secondary in the diet but outnumber the mammals in the diet by diversity, as more than 250 species have been killed in North America alone. Statistically, the most significant avian prey seems to be [[Galliformes|galliforms]], of which they are known to have preyed on 23 species, basically consisting of all of the native species found in the [[United States]].<ref name= Bent/><ref name= Houston/> In the [[Upper Midwest]], the [[ring-necked pheasant]] (''Phasianus colchicus'') and [[northern bobwhite]] (''Colinus virginianus'') were the fifth and sixth (out of 124 identified species) most significant prey species in 4838 pellets.<ref name= Hamerstrom/> Errington characterized the predatory pressure exerted on bobwhites by great horned owls as "light but continuous pressure", which may be considered characteristic of the species' hunting of all galliforms.<ref name= Errington2>{{cite journal |last1=Errington |first1=P. L. |year=1932 |title=Food habits of southern Wisconsin raptors. Part I. Owls |journal=The Condor |volume=34 |issue=4 |pages=176–186 |doi=10.2307/1363563|jstor=1363563 }}</ref>

Usually coveys of quail are partially protected by spending the night roosting communally in dense thickets but should a hunting owl be able to track down the communal roost, losses can be fairly heavy until the roost relocates.<ref name="Craighead" /> Similarly, owls may track down sleeping [[grouse]], which also roost in vegetation but more openly than quail. Some grouse, such as [[greater prairie-chicken]] (''Tympanuchus cupido''), [[sharp-tailed grouse]] (''Tympanuchus phasianellus'') and [[greater sage-grouse]] (''Centrocercus urophasianus''),  may also been vulnerable to great horned owls while displaying conspicuously in openings on a [[Lek mating|lek]] first thing in the morning.<ref name="Hamerstrom2">{{cite journal |last1=Hamerstrom |first1=F. N. |year=1939 |title=A study of Wisconsin prairie chicken and sharp-tailed grouse |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=51 |issue=2 |pages=105–120 |jstor=4156820}}</ref> In the boreal forest, especially in years where the snowshoe hare experiences population decreases, great horned owls prey fairly heavily (approximately 25% of biomass) on [[ruffed grouse]] (''Bonasa umbellus'') and [[spruce grouse]] (''Falcipennis canadensis''), enough so in the earlier bird to possibly contribute to population reductions.<ref name="Adamcik" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Keith |first1=L. B. |first2=D. H. |last2=Rusch |year=1988 |title=Predation's role in the cyclic fluctuations of Ruffed Grouse |journal=Proceeding International Congress of Ornithology |volume=19 |pages=699–732 |url=http://www.barbau.ca/content/predations-role-cyclic-fluctuations-ruffed-grouse }}{{Dead link|date=June 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> Larger species of galliform are not immune to predation either. On [[Protection Island (Washington)|Protection Island]] in [[Washington (state)|Washington state]], introduced [[common peafowl]] (''Pavo cristatus'') are an important prey item.<ref name="Hayward">{{cite journal |last1=Hayward |first1=J. L. |first2=J. G. |last2=Galusha |first3=G. |last3=Frias |year=1993 |title=Analysis of Great Horned Owl pellets with Rhinoceros Auklet remains |journal=The Auk |volume=110 |issue=1 |pages=133–135 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/25221}}</ref> The [[wild turkey]] (''Meleagris gallopavo''), {{convert|4|to|8|kg|lb|abbr=on}} on average between the sexes, is probably the largest bird the great horned owl hunts in which they kill adults. Both full-grown wild turkeys<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schemnitz |first1=S. D. |first2=D. L. |last2=Goerndt |first3=H. |last3=Jones |year=1985 |title=Habitat needs and management of Merriam's turkeys in southcentral New Mexico |journal=Proceedings of National Wild Turkey Symposium |volume=5 |pages=199–232}}</ref> and adult domestic turkeys<ref name="Bent" /> have been hunted and killed. Under normal circumstances, [[Chicken|domestic chicken]] (''Gallus gallus domesticus'') will be ignored in favor of wild prey. On occasion, individual owls, especially inexperienced juveniles, will become habitual fowl killers. These errant owls mainly hunt chickens, though will also take domestic [[guineafowl]], turkeys and anything else available.<ref name="Hamerstrom" /> In general, chickens kept in locked cages with enclosed tops overnight are safe from great horned owls; not so chickens left free range or in open enclosures.<ref name="Bent" />

While galliforms are widely reported, the few cases where great horned owls locally turn to birds as the primary food source over mammals, these may often be local responses to the abundance of breeding [[water bird]]s or concentrations of roosting water birds, since they tend to roost in relatively open spots. They have been known to predate more than 110 different species of assorted water bird.<ref name= Voous/> In prairie wetlands of [[North Dakota]], avian prey, primarily represented by [[duck]]s and the [[American coot]] (''Fulica americana'') came to represent 65% by number and 83% by biomass of the diet of the local owls, also including secondarily [[grebe]]s, smaller [[Rail (bird)|rails]], [[loon]]s, [[Wader|shorebird]]s and [[seabird]]s, as well as upland-based species like [[grey partridge]] (''Perdix perdix''), [[sharp-tailed grouse]] and [[passerine]]s.<ref name=PF>{{cite web | url=https://www.peregrinefund.org/explore-raptors-species/owls/great-horned-owl | title=Great Horned Owl | the Peregrine Fund }}</ref> 77% of the ducks in that study were juveniles, the largest duck being a male [[mallard]] (''Anas platyrhnychos'') weighing approximately {{convert|1250|g|lb|abbr=on}}, but nearly all the coots were adults.<ref name= Murphy/> On [[Protection Island (Washington)|Protection Island]], [[Washington (state)|Washington]], where they are no native land mammals, [[rhinoceros auklet]]s (''Cerorhinca monocerata''), both adults and nestlings, were the most numerous prey, present in 93% of 120 pellets.<ref name= Hayward/> Species as large as adult [[Canada goose|Canada geese]], [[snow goose]]<ref name= Bent/><ref name= Hamerstrom/> and [[great blue heron]]s<ref name= Houston/> have been successfully killed. Medium-sized birds of prey (even other species of owl) are also taken.<ref>König, Claus, Friedhelm Weick, and Jan-Hendrik Becking. Owls of the World. 2nd ed. Bloomsbury Publishing, 2009. Web. 23 May. 2023.</ref> The nestlings of even larger species like [[trumpeter swan]]s (''Cygnus buccinator''),<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Monnie |first1=J. B. |year=1966 |title=Reintroduction of the Trumpeter Swan to its former prairie breeding range |journal=Journal of Wildlife Management |volume=30 |issue=4 |pages=691–696 |doi=10.2307/3798274|jstor=3798274 }}</ref> [[American white pelican]]s (''Pelecanus eryhtrorhynchos''),<ref>{{cite book |last1=Knopf |first1=F. L. |first2=R. M. |last2=Evans |year=2020 |chapter=American White Pelican (''Pelecanus erythrorhynchos''), version 1.0 |title=Birds of the World |editor-first1=A. F. |editor-last1=Poole |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |location=Ithaca, NY |doi=10.2173/bow.amwpel.01|s2cid=216485857 }}</ref> [[brown pelican]]s (''Pelecanus occidentalis'')<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schreiber |first1=R. W. |first2=R. W. |last2=Risebrough |year=1972 |title=Studies of the Brown Pelican |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=84 |issue=2 |pages=119–135 |jstor=4160189}}</ref> and [[sandhill crane]]s (''Grus canadensis'')<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Littlefield |first1=C. D. |year=2003 |title=Sandhill Crane nesting success and productivity in relation to predator removal in southeastern Oregon |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=115 |issue=3 |pages=263–269 |doi=10.1676/02-108 |jstor=4164569|s2cid=86244240 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/210428 }}</ref> have also been killed by these owls.

Other assorted birds are taken seemingly at random opportunity. The predatory effect of this species on other raptorial birds, which is often considerable, is explored in the following section. In [[Brazil]], it was found in a small study that birds overall outnumbered mammals in pellets, although most were not determined to species and the ones that were shown a tremendously diverse assemblage of birds with no obvious dietary preference.<ref name= Tomazzoni>{{cite journal |last1=Tomazzoni |first1=A. C. |last2=Pedó |first2=E. |last3=Hartz |first3=S. M. |year=2004 |title=Food habits of Great Horned Owls (Bubo virginianus) in the breeding season in Lami Biological Reserve, southern Brazil |journal=Ornitologia Neotropical |volume=15 |issue=2 |pages=279–282 |s2cid=90637198 }}</ref> Although not usually numerically significant, 86 species of [[passerine]] have been taken by great horned owls. Members from most North American families are known as prey, although among smaller types such as [[chickadee]]s, [[New World warbler|warbler]]s, [[New World sparrow|sparrow]]s, [[Cardinalidae|cardinalids]], [[wren]]s and most [[tyrant flycatcher]]s only a few species from each have been recorded. Nonetheless, an occasionally unlucky migrant or local breeder is sometimes snatched.<ref name= Voous/><ref name= Bent/><ref name= Hamerstrom/> Fledgling songbirds are regularly taken in spring and summer.<ref name=DwightSmith/> The smallest avian prey known for great horned owls are the {{convert|5.8|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[blue-grey gnatcatcher]] (''Polioptila caerulea'') and the {{convert|6.2|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[ruby-crowned kinglet]] (''Regulus calendula'').<ref name= Hamerstrom/> Somewhat larger bodied families are more prominent, i.e. the [[corvid]]s (14 species) and [[icterid]]s (14 species) and, secondarily, the [[kingbird]]s (''Tyrannus'' ssp.), [[Thrush (bird)|thrushes]], [[mimid]]s and [[European starling]] (''Sturnus vulgaris'').<ref name= Voous/><ref name= Bent/><ref name= Hamerstrom/> This is likely due to the fact that the larger passerines usually roost in relatively open spots and have larger, more conspicuous nests. [[Crow]]s and [[raven]]s tend to be grabbed off of their communal roosts by night.<ref name= Houston/>

===Other prey===
The great horned owl rarely misses an opportunity to hunt [[reptile]]s and [[amphibian]]s. However, [[lizard]]s are largely unavailable as prey due to their typically diurnal periods of activity.<ref name= Houston/> However, some snakes are partially or largely nocturnal, and more than a dozen species are hunted in North America. Snakes hunted range from small, innocuous [[garter snake]]s (''Thamnophis'' ssp.) and [[night snake]]s (''Hypsiglena torquata'') to venomous species like [[Agkistrodon piscivorus|cottonmouth]]s (''Agkistrodon piscivorus'') and [[Crotalus viridis|prairie rattlesnake]]s (''Crotalus virdis'') and formidable, large species like [[Lampropeltis getula|common king snake]]s (''Lampropeltis getula'') and [[Western rat snake|black rat snake]]s (''Pantherophis obsoletus''), which in mature specimens can rival the owl in mass and sheer predatory power.<ref name= Bent/> The capture of the hatchlings of very large reptiles such as [[loggerhead turtle]]s (''Caretta caretta'') and [[American alligator]]s (''Alligator mississippiensis'') by great horned owls has been reported, in both cases likely when the baby reptiles are attempting to make their way to the security of water.<ref name= Bent/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Toland |first1=B. |year=1991 |title=Great horned owl predation of Atlantic loggerhead turtle hatchlings |journal=Florida Field Naturalist |volume=19 |issue= |pages=117–119 |url=https://sora-dev.unm.edu/node/134434}}</ref> On rare occasion, [[salamander]]s, [[frog]]s and [[toad]]s are reported as prey.  On rare occasions, fish are taken including [[goldfish]] (''Carassius auratus''), [[bluegill]] (''Lepomis macrochirus''), [[Ameiurus|bullhead]]s (''Ameiurus'' ssp.), other [[catfish]], [[Catostomidae|suckers]], [[Centrarchidae|sunfish]], [[eel]]s and [[Cyprinidae|dace and chub]].<ref name= Bent/><ref name=DwightSmith/>

Many types of invertebrates are recorded as prey. These include mainly insects, but also [[crayfish]], [[crab]]s, [[centipede]]s, [[spider]]s, [[scorpion]]s and [[worm]]s.<ref name=PF/> The occasionally invertebrate prey taken largely consists of common, large insects such as various [[beetle]]s, [[crickets]], [[grasshopper]]s, [[Nepomorpha|water bug]]s and [[Tettigoniidae|katydid]]s, some of which the great horned owl has even reportedly caught via "hawking", i.e. swooping at on the wing.<ref name="Bent" /><ref name="Houston" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Duncan |first1=J.R. |last2=Lane |first2=P.A. |year=1998 |title=Great Horned Owl Observed Hawking 'Insects' |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=22 |issue=3 |page=93 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/53103}}</ref> In some cases, the content of insects in great horned owl pellets may actually be due to the owls eating other birds which have freshly eaten insects in their own stomachs.<ref name="Hamerstrom" /> It is commonly believed that routine insectivory in great horned owls is mainly restricted to inexperienced young owls too unskilled to graduate to larger prey yet. It is clearly inefficient for owls of this size to attempt to raise young on a diet of foods as small as insects.<ref name="Marti" /> Although rare, [[carrion]]-feeding has been recorded in great horned owls, especially ones wintering in Canada and other northern areas during harsh weather.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Errington |first1=P. L. |year=1938 |title=The Great Horned Owl as an indicator of vulnerability in prey populations |journal=The Journal of Wildlife Management |volume=2 |issue=4 |pages=190–205 |doi=10.2307/3795666|jstor=3795666 }}</ref> Road kills are sometimes opportunistically eaten. A case of an owl scavenging a [[white-tailed deer]] (''Odocoileus virginianus'') carcass, ultimately tearing off the deer's leg, was captured on a motion capture video camera set out to film wildlife.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://www.twincitiesnaturalist.com/2011/03/great-horned-owl-on-motion-camera-birds.html |title=Great Horned Owl on Motion Camera |author=Mona, K. |publisher=Twin Cities Naturalist |access-date=2016-11-29}}</ref>

===Urban vs rural diet===
Studies comparing the diets of rural and urban great horned owls have identified that the most abundant rodent prey in their environment fulfils the majority of their diet.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Hindmarch|first1=Sofi|last2=Elliott|first2=John E.|date=2015-02-04|title=Comparing the diet of Great Horned Owls (''Bubo virginianus'') in rural and urban areas of southwestern British Columbia|journal=The Canadian Field-Naturalist|volume=128|issue=4|page=393|doi=10.22621/cfn.v128i4.1634|issn=0008-3550|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last=Kopij|first=G|date=2012|title=Food niche overlap in co-existing Barn Owl Tyto alba (Scopoli 1769) and Great Horned Owl Bubo virginianus Gmelin 1788 in intensively used farmland|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/287217024|journal=Pol. J. Ecol|volume=61|pages=179–181}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Rullman|first1=Stan|last2=Marzluff|first2=John M.|date=2014|title=Raptor Presence Along an Urban–Wildland Gradient: Influences of Prey Abundance and Land Cover|journal=Journal of Raptor Research|volume=48|issue=3|pages=257–272|doi=10.3356/jrr-13-32.1|s2cid=86034385|issn=0892-1016|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Chace|first1=Jameson F.|last2=Walsh|first2=John J.|date=2006|title=Urban effects on native avifauna: a review|url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S016920460400146X|journal=Landscape and Urban Planning|language=en|volume=74|issue=1|pages=46–69|doi=10.1016/j.landurbplan.2004.08.007|bibcode=2006LUrbP..74...46C |url-access=subscription}}</ref> A study of food niche overlap between closely nested barn and great horned owls living in rural north-eastern Oregon identified voles as by far the most common prey.<ref name=":3" /> In southwestern [[British Columbia]], [[Townsend's vole]]s were the most common prey species, while consumption of rats increased as the nesting location became more urban and rats replaced voles as the most abundant and stable food source.<ref name=":1" />  A similar focus on rats was found in populations in urban parks in [[Seattle]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last=Lambert|first=Anthony|date=1981|title=Presence and Food Preferences of the Great Horned Owl in the Urban Parks of Seattle|journal=The Murrelet|volume=62|issue=1|pages=2–5|doi=10.2307/3534439|jstor=3534439}}</ref>  Although a stable and highly abundant food source, a diet consisting of primarily rats can be harmful to urban great horned owls due to [[bioaccumulation]] of [[rodenticide]]s.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Stone|first1=Ward B.|last2=Okoniewski|first2=Joseph C.|last3=Stedelin|first3=James R.|title=Poisoning of Wildlife with Anticoagulant Rodenticides in New York|date=April 1999|journal=Journal of Wildlife Diseases|volume=35|issue=2|pages=187–193|doi=10.7589/0090-3558-35.2.187|pmid=10231745|s2cid=22823380|issn=0090-3558}}</ref>

===Interspecific predatory relationships===
[[File:Hawk eating prey.jpg|thumb|right|An immature red-tailed hawk eats a vole, one of the many prey items that feed both the competing hawks and great horned owls.]]
Due to their very broad dietary habits, the great horned owls share their prey with many other predators, including avian, mammalian and reptilian ones. Almost every study comparing the diets of North American owls illustrates the considerable overlap in the dietary selection of these species, as all species, besides the primarily insectivorous varieties, rely on many of the same small rodent species for most of their diet, extending from the small northern saw-whet owl and [[eastern screech owl]] to the great horned and great grey owls.<ref name= Marti/><ref name= Cromrich/>

In a long-term study of a block of [[Michigan]], all nine species of accipitrid, falcon and owl that stayed to breed there were found to be primarily dependent on the same two rodent genera, the [[meadow vole]] and the two common ''[[Peromyscus]]'' species.<ref name= Craighead/> In the [[Great Basin]], the owls share black-tailed jackrabbit and [[desert cottontail]] as the primary prey with golden eagles, red-tailed hawks and [[ferruginous hawk]]s (''Buteo regalis''); all four species had diets with more than 90% of the biomass is made up of those lagomorphs. Of these, the great horned owl and golden eagle were able to nest most closely to one another because they had the most strongly dissimilar periods of activity.<ref name= Smith/><ref name= Smith3/> In California, when compared to the local red-tailed hawks and [[Crotalus atrox|western diamondback rattlesnake]]s (''Crotalus atrox''), the diets were most similar in that by number about 15–20% of all three species' diets depended on [[Cottontail rabbit|cottontail]]s, but the largest portion was made up of [[ground squirrel]]s in the hawk and the rattlesnake and desert woodrats and other assorted [[rodent]]s in the great horned owl.<ref name= Fitch/> In the [[Taiga|boreal forests]], the great horned owl's prolificacy as a [[snowshoe hare]] hunter places it second only to the [[Canada lynx]] (''Lynx canadensis'') among all predators. Although locally dependent on the hares as their main food, [[northern goshawk]]s (''Accipiter gentilis''), red-tailed hawks and golden eagles apparently do not have as large of an impact on the hares, nor do mammalian carnivore generalists that also kill many hares, like the [[Fisher (animal)|fisher]], [[bobcat]], [[wolverine]] (''Gulo gulo''), [[coyote]] and larger varieties (i.e. [[Gray wolf|wolves]] (''Canis lupus''), [[cougar]]s (''Puma concolor'') and [[bear]]s (''Ursus'' ssp.)).<ref name= Rohner/><ref name= Houston2/><ref name= Mcinvaille/><ref>WLM. (1918). ''Bird Enemies of the Varying Hare''. The Auk, 372–373.</ref>

The relationship between great horned owls and other raptorial birds in its range is usually decidedly one-sided. While certain species, such as the red-tailed hawk and [[northern goshawk]], might be seen as potential competition for the owls, most others seem to be regarded merely as prey by great horned owls. The great horned owl is both the most prolific and diverse predator in America of other birds of prey, with other accomplished raptor-hunters such as the goshawk and the golden eagle being more restricted in range, habitat and number in North America and thus having a more minor impact. All studies have found raptors are a small portion of this owl's diet but predation can be seriously detrimental for such prey, as raptors tend to be territorial and sparsely distributed as a rule and thus can be effectively decimated by a small number of losses.<ref name= Voous/><ref name= Cromrich/> In the [[gray hawk]] (''Buteo plagiatus''), for example, in a study of one breeding block of [[Arizona]], the owls were observed to visit nests nightly until all the nestlings were gone.<ref>Bibles, Brent D., Richard L. Glinski and R. Roy Johnson. 2002. ''Gray Hawk (Buteo plagiatus)'', The Birds of North America Online (A. Poole, Ed.). Ithaca: Cornell Lab of Ornithology.</ref>

Raptorial birds in general tend to have large, conspicuous nests which may make them easier for a hunting owl to locate. The great horned owl gains an advantage by nesting earlier than any other raptor in its range (indeed any bird), as it is able to exploit the other raptors as food while in a more vulnerable state as their own nestlings have become well developed.<ref name= Craighead/> On average, great horned owls begin nesting about three weeks before red-tailed hawks begin to build nests, although some raptors may locally breed as much as two months after the owls.<ref name= Houston/><ref name= Dunstan>Dunstan, T. C., & Harrell, B. E. (1973). ''Spatio-temporal relationships between breeding red-tailed hawks and great horned owls in South Dakota''. Raptor Research, 7(2), 49–54.</ref>

More so than diurnal varieties of raptor, fairly significant numbers of owls are hunted, as all species are to some extent nocturnal and thus their corresponding activity can attract the horned owl's unwanted attention. The extent of predation on other owls depends on the habitat preferences of the other species. Eastern and western screech owls may be most vulnerable since they prefer similar wooded edge habitat. In a block of [[Wisconsin]], great horned owls were responsible for the failure of 78% of eastern screech owl's nests.<ref name= Craighead/> Long-eared owls and, to a lesser extent, barn owls tend to hunt in open, sparsely treed habitats more so than great horned owls, but since they may return to wooded spots for nesting purposes, they may be more vulnerable there. The long-eared owl and barn owls are often compared to the great horned owl as these medium-sized species often occur in abutting habitats and often hunt primarily the same vole and mice species, although the alternate prey of the great horned tends to be much larger, including the smaller owls themselves.<ref name= Earhart/><ref name= Rudolph/><ref>Barrows, C. W. (1989). Diets of five species of desert owls. Western Birds, 20(1), 1–10.</ref> In a pair of studies from [[Colorado]], the average weight of prey for long-eared owls was {{convert|28|to|30|g|oz|abbr=on}}, {{convert|46|to|57.1|g|oz|abbr=on}} for barn owl and {{convert|177|to|220|g|oz|abbr=on}} for the great horned owl.<ref name= Marti/><ref name= Marti2/>

Both young and adult great grey owls, despite their superficially superior size, are apparently hunted with impunity by great horned owls. In the boreal forests, both the northern hawk owl and great grey owl appear to be in greater danger of great horned owl predation in years where the snowshoe hare have low populations.<ref name= Duncan>Duncan, J. R. 1987. ''Movement strategies, mortality, and behavior of radio-marked Great Gray Owls in southeastern Manitoba and northern Minnesota''. Pages 101–107 in Biology and conservation of northern forest owls: Symposium proceedings, February 3–7, Winnipeg, MB. (Nero, R. W., R. J. Clark, R. J. Knapton, and R. H. Hamre, Eds.) General Technical Report, RM-142. Fort Collins, CO: USDA, Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Forest and Range Experiment Station.</ref><ref>Rohner, C., Smith, J. N., Stroman, J., Joyce, M., Doyle, F. I., & Boonstra, R. (1995). ''Northern hawk-owls in the nearctic boreal forest: prey selection and population consequences of multiple prey cycles''. Condor, 208–220.</ref> Great horned owls were the leading cause of mortality in juvenile spotted owls (30% of losses) and juvenile great grey owls (65% of losses).<ref name= Duncan/><ref>Forsman, E. D., Anthony, R. G., Reid, J. A., Loschl, P. J., Sovern, S. G., Taylor, M., & Seaman, D. E. (2002). ''Natal and breeding dispersal of northern spotted owls''. Wildlife Monographs, 1–35.</ref> Less is known about relations with the snowy owl, which may compete with great horned owls for food while invading south for the winter. Anecdotally, both snowy and great horned owls have rarely been reported to dominate or even kill one another depending on the size and disposition of the individual owls, although the snowy's preference for more open areas again acts as something of a buffer. The snowy may be the one North American owl too formidable for the great horned owl to consider as prey.<ref name= Potapov/>

Whereas owls of any age are freely attacked by great horned owls whether nesting or not, when it comes to diurnal raptors, great horned owls are mainly a danger around the nest. They often hunt diurnal raptors when they come across their often relatively conspicuous active platform nests during hunting forays in spring and summer, taking numbers of both nestlings and brooding adults.<ref name= Houston/> Again, like owls, diurnal raptors are attacked depending on the relative similarity of their habitat preferences to the owl. [[Cooper's hawk]]s (''Accipiter cooperii'') and red-tailed hawks tend to be most vulnerable, as they prefer the same wooded edges frequented by great horned owls. Other diurnal raptors may be attracted to more enclosed wooded areas, such as [[sharp-shinned hawk]]s (''Accipiter striatus'') or [[zone-tailed hawk]]s (''Buteo albonotatus''), or more open plain and meadow areas, such as [[Northern harrier]]s (''Circus hudsonius'') and ferruginous hawks, but this is almost never a total insurance against predation as all of these are recorded prey.<ref name= Hamerstrom/><ref>Stoleson, S. H., & Sadoti, G. (2010). ''Zone-tailed Hawk (Buteo albonotatus)''. In: Cartron, Jean-Luc, ed. Raptors of New Mexico. Albuquerque, NM: University of New Mexico Press.</ref><ref>Sutherland, J. E. 1987. ''The predation ecology of the Northern Harrier (Circus cyaneus hudsonius) on Mallard Island, North Dakota''. Master's Thesis. Univ. of N. Dakota, Grand Forks.</ref><ref>Bechard, Marc J. and Josef K. Schmutz. 1995. ''Ferruginous Hawk (Buteo regalis)'', The Birds of North America Online (A. Poole, Ed.). Ithaca: Cornell Lab of Ornithology.</ref>

In a study of [[red-shouldered hawk]] (''Buteo lineatus'') and [[broad-winged hawk]] (''Buteo brachyurus'') breeding in [[New York (state)|New York]], despite their nesting in deeper woods than those that host these owls, the main cause of nest failure was great horned owl predation.<ref name= Crocoll/> Similarly, the great horned owl was the primary cause of nesting failure for both desert-dwelling [[Harris's hawk]]s (''Parabuteo unicinctus'') and forest-dwelling [[northern goshawk]] in [[Arizona]] (39% and 40% of failures, respectively), wetland-inhabiting [[osprey]] (''Pandion haliaetus'') in [[Delaware]] (21% of failures) and [[peregrine falcon]]s (''Falco peregrinus'') in the [[Western United States]] (27% of failures).<ref>Dawson, J. W., & Mannan, R. W. (1991). ''The role of territoriality in the social organization of Harris' Hawks''. The Auk, 661–672.</ref><ref>Steidl, R. J., & Griffin, C. R. (1991). ''Growth and brood reduction of mid-Atlantic coast ospreys''. The Auk, 363–370.</ref><ref>Walton, B. J., & Thelander, C. G. (1988). ''Peregrine falcon management efforts in California, Oregon, Washington, and Nevada''. Peregrine falcon populations: their management and recovery. The Peregrine Fund, Boise, Idaho, 587–598</ref><ref>Boal, C. W., & Mannan, R. W. (1994). ''Northern goshawk diets in ponderosa pine forests on the Kaibab Plateau''. Studies in Avian Biology, 16, 97–102.</ref> The fact that many of the nests great horned owls use are constructed by accipitrids may lead to localized conflicts, almost always to the detriment of the hawks rather than the owls. While the young of larger diurnal raptors are typically stolen in the night, great horned owls also readily kill large adult raptors both in and out of breeding seasons, including [[osprey]], [[northern goshawk]] and [[rough-legged buzzard]].<ref>Cold, C. W. 1993. ''Adult male osprey killed at nest by Great Horned Owl''. Passenger Pigeon 55:269–270.</ref><ref>Rohner, C. and F. I. Doyle. 1992. ''Food-stressed Great Horned Owl kills adult goshawk: exceptional observation or community process?'' Journal of Raptor Research, 26:261–263.</ref><ref>Bechard, Marc J. and Theodor R. Swem. 2002. ''Rough-legged Hawk (Buteo lagopus)'', The Birds of North America Online (A. Poole, Ed.). Ithaca: Cornell Lab of Ornithology.</ref>

Great horned owls are frequently mobbed by other birds. Most accipitrids will readily mob them, as will falcons. [[Hen harrier]]s, [[northern goshawk]]s, [[Cooper's hawk]]s, [[Harris's hawk]]s, red-tailed hawks, [[Swainson's hawk]]s, [[ferruginous hawk]]s, [[red-shouldered hawk]]s, [[American kestrel]]s, [[peregrine falcon]]s, [[prairie falcon]]s (''Falco mexicanus'') and [[common raven]]s (''Corvus corax'') are among the reported species who have been recorded diving on great horned owls when they discover them.<ref name= Houston/><ref name= Orians/><ref name= Rohner3>Rohner, C., Krebs, C. J., Hunter, D. B., & Currie, D. C. (2000). ''Roost site selection of great horned owls in relation to black fly activity: an anti-parasite behavior? '' The Condor, 102(4), 950–955.</ref><ref>Houston, C. S. and K. A. Wylie. 1985. Peregrine Falcons harass nesting Great Horned Owls. Blue Jay 43:42–43.</ref> In [[Arizona]] and [[Texas]], they may be mobbed by [[Mexican jay]]s and [[western scrub jay]]s (''Aphelocoma wollweberi & californica'') and [[Western kingbird|western]] and [[Cassin's kingbird]]s (''Tyrannus verticalis & vociferans'').<ref>Cully, J. F., & Ligon, J. D. (1976). ''Comparative mobbing behavior of scrub and Mexican jays''. The Auk, 116–125.</ref><ref>Ohlendorf, H. M. (1974). ''Competitive relationships among kingbirds (Tyrannus) in trans-Pecos Texas''. Wilson Bulletin, 86(4), 357–373.</ref> In addition, there are several documented incidences of [[American crow]]s mobbing a great horned owl, in groups of dozens or even hundreds of crows.<ref name= Bent/> In response to mobbing, if the owl flies it alights to the nearest secluded spot. If an owl alights on ground or on exposed branch or ledge, it may respond to swooping and stooping flights of corvids and raptors with threat display and raising of its wings.<ref name= Houston/>

==Reproduction==
[[File:Great horned owl chick 3w.jpg|thumb|left|Nestlings of the Rocky Mountains great horned owl (''B. v. pinorum'') in [[New Mexico]]]]
[[File:Bubo virginianus -near Tule Lake National Wildlife Refuge, Oregan, USA -juvenile-8.jpg|right|thumb|Juvenile coastal great horned owls (''B. v. saturatus'') near [[Tule Lake National Wildlife Refuge]], Oregon, United States]]
Great horned owls are some of the earliest-breeding birds in North America, seemingly in part because of the lengthy nightfall at this time of year and additionally the competitive advantage it gives the owl over other raptors. In most of North America, courtship is from October to December and mates are chosen by December to January.<ref name=Konig/> This species was once thought to be strictly monogamous, but recent analysis indicates one male may mate with two females simultaneously, as was discovered for the first time in 2018 in Reno, Nevada.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.nationalgeographic.com/animals/article/animals-owls-birds-parenting-polygamy | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180503042406/https://news.nationalgeographic.com/2018/04/animals-owls-birds-parenting-polygamy/#close | archive-date=May 3, 2018 | title=These Owl Chicks Have Two Moms and a Dad – a First| website=[[National Geographic Society]] | date=2018-05-02}}</ref> During courtship in late fall or early winter, the male attracts the attention of his mate by hooting emphatically while leaning over (with the tail folded or cocked) and puffing up his white throat to look like a ball.<ref name= Konig/> The white throat may serve as a visual stimulus in the low light conditions typical of when this owl courts.<ref name= Rohner/> He often flies up and down on a perch, while approaching the potential mate. Eventually, he comes to approach the female and tries to rub his bill against hers while repeatedly bowing. If receptive, the female hoots back when the pair meet but is more subdued in both her hoot and display. The male may convince the female by bringing her freshly caught prey, which they will share.<ref name=Bent/><ref name= Konig/> While males often hoot emphatically for about a month or six weeks towards the end of the year, the period where females also hoot is usually only a week to ten days.<ref name= Baumgartner/> Pairs typically breed together year after year and may mate for life, although they associate with each other more loosely when their young become mostly independent.<ref name= Konig/> Pairs rekindling their reproductive relationship in the winter may perform a milder courtship to strengthen pair bonds before producing young.<ref name= Baumgartner/>

Males select nesting sites and bring the females' attention to them by flying to the nest and then stomping on it.<ref name=Konig/> Considering the owls' large size, nests with open access are preferred to those enclosed with surrounding branches. Like all owls, great horned owls do not build their own nest. Great horned owls tend to examine an area for an abandoned nest, generally from larger birds like hawks, and take over the nest for raising their own young.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Morse|first=Douglass H.|date=April 1971|title=Great Horned Owls and Nesting Seabirds|journal=The Auk|volume=88|issue=2|pages=426–427|doi=10.2307/4083889|jstor=4083889|issn=0004-8038}}</ref> They nest in a wider variety of sites than any other North American bird.<ref name= Baumgartner/> Many nests are in cavernous hollows of dead trees or their branches, and especially in southern states in large trees along the edge of old-growth lots.<ref name= Baumgartner/> In mountainous or hilly areas, especially in canyons of the [[Southwestern United States|Southwest]] and [[Rocky Mountains]], cliff ledges, small caves, and other sheltered depressions may be used.<ref name= Baumgartner/> Owls living in prairie country, in the absence of other animals' nests, riparian tree-hollows or man-made structures, will use boulders, buttes, railroad cuts, low bushes and even the bare ground as nest sites.<ref name= Baumgartner/> Ground nests have also been recorded in the midst of tall grasses in [[Florida]] and in the midst of brushy spots on desert ground.<ref name= Voous/> Even the burrow entrances of [[American badger]] and [[coyote]] dens have reportedly been used as nests, in spite of the inherent risk of sharing space with such potentially dangerous co-inhabitants.<ref name= Baumgartner/> Nesting behavior for the great horned owl appears to be more closely related to prey availability than it does to seasonal conditions. There has been some evidence that if prey availability is low enough then the species may forgo mating entirely for a season. Male and female owls of the species have been observed to help incubate the eggs once they have been laid on a nest.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://greathornedowl.net/great-horned-owl-nest/|title=Great Horned Owl Nest – Interesting Facts & Information|last=Waleed|date=2018-08-29|website=Great Horned Owl|language=en-US|access-date=2020-02-21|archive-date=2020-02-21|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200221212620/https://greathornedowl.net/great-horned-owl-nest/|url-status=dead}}</ref>

[[File:Bubo virginianus00.jpg|thumb|right|A brooding female common great horned owl (''B. v. virginianus'') on her nest in [[Louisiana]]]]
Most tree nests used by great horned owls are constructed by other animals, often from a height of about {{convert|4.5|to|22|m|ft|abbr=on}} off the ground. They often take over a nest used by some other large bird, sometimes adding feathers to line the nest but usually not much more. Allegedly there have some cases where the owls have reinforced a nest structure or appeared to have reconstructed a nest, but as a rule no owl species has ever been known to actually build a nest.<ref name= Baumgartner/> Great horned owls in the Southwest may also use nests in [[Cactus|cacti]], built by [[Harris's hawk]] and red-tailed hawk, as well as large hollows in cacti.<ref>Mader, W. J. 1973. ''Notes on nesting Great Horned Owls in southern Arizona''. Raptor Research, 7:109–111.</ref> The nests they use are often made by most larger types of acciptrids, from species as small as [[Cooper's hawk]]s to [[bald eagle]] and golden eagle, though perhaps most often those of red-tailed hawks and other [[Buteoninae|buteonines]]. Secondly in popularity are [[crow]] and [[raven]] (''Corvus'' ssp.) nests. Even [[Canada goose]], [[black-crowned night heron]] (''Nycticorax nycticorax'') and [[great blue heron]] nests have been used, the latter sometimes right in the midst of an active [[heronry]].<ref>Bendire, C. E. 1892. ''Life histories of North American birds with special reference to their breeding habits and eggs''. Smithsonian Contributions to Knowledge 28:376–389.</ref><ref>Burkholder, G. & Smith, D.G. 1988. ''Great Horned Owl (Bubo virginianus) nesting in a Great Blue Heron (Ardea herodias) heronry''. Journal of Raptor Research, 22 (2): 62.</ref> The leaf nests of [[squirrel]]s are also regularly used but in general great horned owls are partial to stick nests since they provide a much firmer, safer foundation.<ref name= Houston/>

The stage at which eggs are laid is variable across North America. In [[Southern Florida]], eggs may be laid as early as late November to as late as early January. In the [[Southeastern United States|southeast]], from south [[Texas]] to [[Georgia (U.S. state)|Georgia]], egg laying may begin from late December to early February. From [[Southern California]] to northern [[Louisiana]], egg laying is from early February to late March. The largest swath of egg-laying owls from central California to [[South Carolina]] to as far north as [[Ohio]] and [[Massachusetts]] is from late February to early April. In the [[Rocky Mountains]], [[Northwestern United States]], northern [[New England]] and eastern Canada, egg laying is from early March to late April. In the rest of Canada and Alaska, egg laying may be from late March to early May.<ref name= Baumgartner/> The latest known date of egg laying was in mid-June in [[Saskatchewan]] and the [[Yukon Territory]].<ref>Smith, D. G. 1969. ''Nesting ecology of the Great Horned Owl, Bubo virginianus''. Brigham Young University Science Bulletin Biological Services, 10(4):16–25.</ref> In northwestern [[Utah]] and north-central [[Alberta]], egg-laying can be 3–4 weeks earlier than usual when food is abundant and weather is favorable.<ref name= Houston/> For owls found in more tropical climates, the dates of the breeding season are somewhat undefined.<ref name= Konig/> There are usually 2 eggs per clutch, but clutches range in size from 1 to 6 eggs (over 3 is uncommon, over 4 is very rare), depending on environmental conditions.<ref>Austing, G. R. 1968. ''The owls and I''. Audubon 70:72–79.</ref><ref>Peck, G. K. and R. D. James. 1983. ''Breeding birds of Ontario: nidiology and distribution''. Vol. 1. Royal Ontario Museum, Toronto.</ref> The average egg width is {{convert|46.5|mm|in|abbr=on}}, the average length is {{convert|55|mm|in|abbr=on}} and the average weight is {{convert|51|g|oz|abbr=on}}, although mass could be slightly higher elsewhere because this figure is from [[Los Angeles County]], [[California|CA]] where the owls are relatively small.<ref name= Turner>Turner, Jr., J. C. and L. McClanahan, Jr. 1981. ''Physiogenesis of endothermy and its relation to growth in the Great Horned Owl, Bubo virginianus''. Compilation of Biochemical Physiology, 68A:167–173.</ref> The incubation period ranges from 28 to 37 days, averaging 33 days.<ref name= Hoffmeister>Hoffmeister, D. F. and H. W. Setzer. 1947. ''The postnatal development of two broods of Great Horned Owls (Bubo virginianus)''. University of Kansas Publishing Museum of Natural History, 1:157–173.</ref> The female alone usually does all the incubation and rarely moves from the nest, while the male owl captures food and brings it to her, with the first nightly food delivery typically occurring soon after dark.<ref name= Houston/>

The young weigh {{convert|34.7|g|oz|abbr=on}} at birth on average and can gain about {{convert|33|g|oz|abbr=on}} a day for the first four weeks of life, with typical weights in the range of {{convert|800|or|1000|g|lb|abbr=on}} by 25–29 days for males and females, respectively.<ref name= Turner/><ref name= Hoffmeister/> When first hatched the young are covered in whitish gray down, with some brownish about the wings. Gradually the soft juvenile downy plumage comes through the down, being typically a cinnamon-buff color, but with variable hues predicting the eventual color of the mature owls. The extent of down gradually diminishes, developing mature-looking plumage by late summer, although many first year birds still have scattered bits of down into autumn. By late autumn, first-year birds look similar to adults but with a slightly warmer, reddish tinge, less well developed ear tufts and a smaller white throat patch.<ref name= Bent/> The nestling owls develop mostly in behavior between two weeks and two months of age, in which time they adapt the ability to defend themselves, grasp foods and climb. Vocally, the young are able to exert weak chirps while still in the egg, developing into a raspy chirp shortly after hatching. The calls of the young increase rapidly in intensity, pitch and character, some juvenile males mimicking their father's hooting in fall but usually they conclude with various odd gurgling notes. The earliest competent hooting by juvenile owls is not until January.<ref name= Houston/><ref>Sumner, Jr., E. L. 1934. ''The behavior of some young raptorial birds''. University of California, Publication on Zoology, 40:331–362.</ref> Young owls move onto nearby branches at 6 weeks and start to fly about a week later.
